EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster/line of thunderstorms moved across southern Wisconsin the evening of March 5th.  Within this line of storms, some briefly intense downburst winds occurred across portions of south central to east central Wisconsin.  A brief tornado also occurred in southeast Dane county.  Quite a bit of structural damage was noted to a number of farming sheds and buildings in areas of Dane, Rock and Fond du Lac counties. Heavy rainfall on a frozen ground also led to some basement flooding and minor street flooding.|A home was struck by lightning in the Town of Deerfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado path started to the southeast of the town of Dunkirk in Dane County.  This tornado went through a farm and destroyed a tobacco barn, part of a silo and peeled back sheet metal on a few buildings on the grounds.  This sheet metal was noted to have been lofted to locations nearly a mile away.  A few other structures had structural damage to them along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. As the storms moved into northwest Wisconsin, they quickly weekend and only produced sporadic wind damage as they raced to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few small trees were down along with a few larger trees that were blocking roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northwestern Wisconsin during the month of October were fairly steady state with just a slight expansion of D2 Severe Drought near Chequamegon Bay during the middle of the month. Overall, precipitation was only about 25-50% of normal for the month, but no significant expansion of D2 Severe Drought was observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across Ashland County during the month of October. Just under an inch of precipitation was recorded for the month at the Ashland airport which was nearly two inches below normal. Despite this only minor expansion of D2 Severe Drought was seen with the update on the 11th. The area of D2 Severe Drought was confined to the far northwestern corner of the county near Chequamegon Bay. No further changes were noted over the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through southern Wisconsin was the focus for tornadic development through the lunch hour.  Temperatures and dew points rose enough in the late morning to help fuel the strengthening of the rain along the front.  There were numerous tornadic debris signatures that developed, many of which appeared to be extremely brief which did no damage outside of lofting leaves into the air.  The main tornado for this event was the one that went through West Allis which mainly did tree damage as it came through.  No lightning was observed through this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was reported in Hartford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained in place to begin January across much of northwestern Wisconsin. The worst areas were along the Bayfield Peninsula and areas near Chequamegon Bay, where D2 Severe Drought persisted. Precipitation overall was generally near to below normal, but deep snowpack and precipitation at the end of December and beginning of January led to the removal of all of the areas of D2 Severe Drought by the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far northern reaches of Iron County remained in D2 Severe Drought to begin the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to below normal, but a deep snowpack and precipitation at the end of December and beginning of January led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought over the area with the update on the 11th, leaving D1 Moderate Drought conditions across the entirety of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures fell to -15 to -20 degrees during the morning of January 10th. The combination of the bitter cold and gusty northwesterly winds created wind chills of -35 to -38 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains to northern Lower Michigan on the 4th into the 5th. This storm track brought an area of heavy snowfall to parts of northwest Wisconsin starting the evening of the 4th before lingering into the late evening hours of the 5th. Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was common from Douglas County eastward to Iron County with lesser amounts to the south. As the low moved away from the region, lake-effect snow continued to pound Bayfield, Ashland and Iron counties with final totals reaching nearly two feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were common across the county with heavier amounts in eastern reaches that received the edge of the lake-effect snow. The highest total was 18 inches at Lake Nebagamon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Approaching low pressure and a surge of relatively mild and moist air aloft brought 1-3 inches of powdery snow for the morning rush hour. Slippery roads led to some vehicle slide-offs and accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band from eastern Lake Superior extended southward over the Bay of Green Bay and into Door County on Thursday (1/6). The band drifted west and remained semi stationary over southwestern Door County through mid-day.  Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 9 inches across southwestern Door County.  The highest snowfall amount was 9 inches in Little Sturgeon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across southwestern Door County was 9 inches in Little Sturgeon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold high pressure that brought widespread wind chills in the 40s below zero the night of the 25th moved to the east of the region through the day and into the night of the 26th. While temperatures were a bit warmer, a tighter pressure gradient was in place ahead of an area of low pressure moving into Manitoba. The winds led to wind chill readings again in the 40s below zero, but were not as widespread as the night before. The coldest reading was -46F near Clam Lake.

Drought conditions remained in place to begin January across much of northwestern Wisconsin. The worst areas were along the Bayfield Peninsula and areas near Chequamegon Bay, where D2 Severe Drought persisted. Precipitation overall was generally near to below normal, but deep snowpack and precipitation at the end of December and beginning of January led to the removal of all of the areas of D2 Severe Drought by the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northeastern corner of Bayfield County remained in D2 Severe Drought to begin the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to below normal, but a deep snowpack and precipitation at the end of December and beginning of January led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought over the area with the update on the 11th, leaving D1 Moderate Drought conditions across the entirety of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving eastward across Lake Superior during the day on the 16th and into the 17th led to northwesterly winds on the backside of the low across western Lake Superior. With colder air arriving behind the low and relatively warm surface water temperatures across western Lake Superior, heavy lake-effect snow developed late on the 16th and continued into the 17th before the low moved far enough away and the winds weakend, ending the lake-effect snow. Snowfall totals as high as 18 were reported along the Penokee Range in northern Iron County during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in the northern Iron County snowbelt received 18 of snow total during the event, with half of it falling during the first 11 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted into the region during the morning hours of the 11th bringing unseasonably warm temperatures into the Northland for the afternoon hours. The associated cold front arrived for the evening and overnight hours and led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. While most remained below severe levels, one storm that moved across Douglas County did produce hail to the size of pennies.

A prolonged snowfall event occurred across northwestern Wisconsin beginning the morning of the 21st and lasting into the late evening hours of the 22nd and early morning hours of the 23rd. A strong area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley during this time period leading to multiple waves of snow that affected the Northland. Most of the region received at least 6 inches of accumulation by the time the storm ended with many areas receiving upwards of 1 foot of accumulation by the time snow ended. The Bayfield Peninsula was particularly hard hit with 33.5 inches piling up near Washburn by the time snow ended. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were seen at times during the morning of the 21st across the Bayfield Peninsula as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 9 to 15 inches were received from across the county with a high report of 15.4 inches near Poplar. The combination of heavy snow and high winds at one point led to closures of both the Bong and Blatnik bridges due to vehicle collisions involving numerous vehicles, however, no injuries were reported with these incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snowfall event occurred across northwestern Wisconsin beginning the morning of the 21st and lasting into the late evening hours of the 22nd and early morning hours of the 23rd. Most of the region received at least 6 inches of accumulation by the time the storm ended with many areas receiving upwards of 1 foot of accumulation by the time snow ended. The Bayfield Peninsula was particularly hard hit with 33.5 inches piling up near Washburn by the time snow ended. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were seen at times during the morning of the 21st across the Bayfield Peninsula as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was prolific across Bayfield County during the event, especially across the Bayfield Peninsula. Snowfall ranged from around 10 inches in the southern part of the county to over 30 inches in the Bayfield Peninsula. The highest report was 33.5 inches just outside of Washburn and at one point early in the event, snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were observed for multiple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure slowly moved from the southern Great Plains into lower MI producing freezing rain and sleet over southern WI.  Most of the ice accumulation was under 1/4 inch with sleet accumulation of 1/2 inch or less. Thunderstorms occurred at times and produced mostly sleet. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred on roadways across southern WI. Widespread school closures occurred due to the icy roads.  Icy sidewalks led to pedestrian falls and injuries throughout southern WI. Ascension Wisconsin reported 42 persons treated from ice related falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of one tenth of an inch or less and sleet accumulation less than one half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls occurred across portions of western Wisconsin during the late afternoon of February 18th as a cold front moved across the region. Snowfall rates around 2 inches an hour, wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and visibilities under a mile occurred with the snow squalls, which mainly impacted the Interstate 94 corridor. Roads quickly became snow covered despite only a half to one inch of snow accumulation. Northwest winds of 40 to 60 mph buffeted the area behind the cold front as well. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse (La Crosse County) airport measured a gust of 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A brief period of heavy snow occurred across Monroe County as a cold front moved across the region. In the snow squalls, snowfall rates were around two inches an hour, the wind gusted to around 40 mph and the visibility was reduced to under a mile. The snow squall was followed by a period of 40 to 50 mph wind gusts into the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Northland during the early and mid-afternoon hours of the 18th. Heavy snow showers developed along the front and produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph leading to snow squalls in spots that reduced visibilities to under a quarter of a mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph along with heavy snowfall moved from west to east across northern Douglas County during the early afternoon hours. Visibilities rapidly dropped to a quarter of a mile or less with rapid snow accumulations on roadways per webcams in the area. Travel was especially difficult along US2 and US53, especially in the Superior area, as the snow squall passed through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing drizzle occurred with the passage of an arctic front, mainly over southeast WI and portions of south central WI from the late evening into the overnight hours. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred as traffic increased during the morning rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice on roads and sidewalks from a brief period of freezing drizzle.

Travel was especially difficult along US2 as the snow squall passed through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Northland during the early and mid-afternoon hours of the 18th. Heavy snow showers developed along the front and produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph leading to snow squalls in spots that reduced visibilities to under a quarter of a mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph along with heavy snowfall moved from west to east across the northern half of Ashland County during the mid-afternoon hours. Visibilities rapidly dropped to a quarter of a mile or less with rapid snow accumulations on roadways per webcams in the area. Travel was especially difficult along US2 as the snow squall passed through with visibilities falling to nearly zero on the east side of Ashland per one webcam view.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing drizzle occurred with the passage of an arctic front, mainly over southeast WI and portions of south central WI from the late evening into the overnight hours. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred as traffic increased during the morning rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice on roads and sidewalks from a brief period of freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls occurred across portions of western Wisconsin during the late afternoon of February 18th as a cold front moved across the region. Snowfall rates around 2 inches an hour, wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and visibilities under a mile occurred with the snow squalls, which mainly impacted the Interstate 94 corridor. Roads quickly became snow covered despite only a half to one inch of snow accumulation. Northwest winds of 40 to 60 mph buffeted the area behind the cold front as well. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse (La Crosse County) airport measured a gust of 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A brief period of heavy snow occurred across Jackson County as a cold front moved across the region. In the snow squalls, snowfall rates were around two inches an hour, the wind gusted to around 40 mph and the visibility was reduced to under a mile. The snow squall was followed by a period of 40 to 50 mph wind gusts into the early evening. The automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport measured a wind gust of 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall with the passage of an arctic front brought a brief period of white-out or nearly white-out conditions during the evening. A brief period of heavy snow and wind gusts of 40-50 mph accompanied the squall. A 72 mph wind gust was reported in West Bend as the squall passed. Traffic was significantly stalled or ceased by the squall with some vehicle slide-offs and accidents reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall passage via heavy snow, 40-50 mph wind gusts, and near white-out conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure slowly moved from the southern Great Plains into lower MI producing freezing rain and sleet over southern WI.  Most of the ice accumulation was under 1/4 inch with sleet accumulation of 1/2 inch or less. Thunderstorms occurred at times and produced mostly sleet. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred on roadways across southern WI. Widespread school closures occurred due to the icy roads.  Icy sidewalks led to pedestrian falls and injuries throughout southern WI. Ascension Wisconsin reported 42 persons treated from ice related falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation less than one quarter inch and sleet accumulation one half inch or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent clipper system brought 1 to 2 inches of light snow to the area during the day and evening of Friday (2/18).  A snow squall developed ahead of the arctic cold front associated with the clipper, tracking through central and east-central Wisconsin during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The snow squall caused visibilities to fall to around one quarter of a mile with gusty winds of 30 to 50 mph. Northwest winds gusted to 40 to 60 mph behind the arctic cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibility dropped to one quarter mile for around 9 minutes. Wind gusts of 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to strengthen late Monday night across the central plains in advance of a powerful southern jet stream that intensified over the center part of the country on Tuesday. The center of this storm moved from northern Missouri northeast across the Midweek and eastern Great Lakes region by late Tuesday night.||A large area of light to moderate precipitation developed across southern and central Minnesota early Tuesday morning and moved northeast while heavier precipitation started to develop across south central, and into east central Minnesota, and adjacent west-central Wisconsin Tuesday morning. This secondary area of higher reflectivity was in response to the deepening southern jet and associated upper level forcing. This led to moderate to occasional heavy snowfall across parts of west central Wisconsin. A band of 4 to 10 inches of snow fell from the southeastern Twin Cities metro area, northeast across west central Wisconsin from Red Wing, to Rice Lake and Ladysmith.||Some of the higher snowfall totals include:||9.5 inches near Baldwin.|8.0 inches near Boyceville. |7.7 inches south of River Falls. |7.5 inches near Wheeler. |7.5 inches in Roberts.|7.1 inches in River Falls.|7.0 inches in Chetek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began early Tuesday morning, February 22nd. However, the bulk of the heavier snowfall, higher snowfall rates, and possibly mixed with sleet at times, occurred during the late morning and early afternoon hours. By the time the light snow tapered off, locally 6 to 8 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across northern Illinois brought a swath of heavy snow and gusty northerly winds of 15 to 25 mph during the afternoon hours of Tuesday (2/22). Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 8 inches across portions of north-central Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 7.5 inches in Manitowish Waters (Vilas Co.), 6.8 inches south of Woodruff (Oneida Co.), and 6.2 inches near Woodruff (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 7.5 inches in Manitowish Waters and 6.2 inches near Woodruff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking through the Ohio Valley brought a surface trough through northeast Wisconsin during the evening hours of Thursday (2/24). As the trough tracked to the east, low level winds became favorable for lake effect snow to develop across the Lake Michigan lakeshore during the late evening on Thursday (2/24) and early morning hours of Friday (2/25). Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 10 inches across the lakeshore.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.0 inches in Casco (Kewaunee Co.) as well as Point Beach State Forest (Manitowoc Co.), 9.5 inches in Algoma (Kewaunee Co.), 8.0 inches in Mishicot (Manitowoc Co.), and 7 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across Vilas County included 7.0 inches near Ephraim.

Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 10 inches across the lakeshore.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.0 inches in Casco (Kewaunee Co.) as well as Point Beach State Forest (Manitowoc Co.), 9.5 inches in Algoma (Kewaunee Co.), 8.0 inches in Mishicot (Manitowoc Co.), and 7 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Kewaunee County included 10.0 inches in Casco, 9.5 inches in Algoma, and 6.3 inches near Kewaunee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall with the passage of an arctic front brought a brief period of white-out or nearly white-out conditions during the evening. A brief period of heavy snow and wind gusts of 40-50 mph accompanied the squall. A 72 mph wind gust was reported in West Bend as the squall passed. Traffic was significantly stalled or ceased by the squall with some vehicle slide-offs and accidents reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall passage via heavy snow, 40-50 mph wind gusts, and near white-out conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to strengthen late Monday night across the central plains in advance of a powerful southern jet stream that intensified over the center part of the country on Tuesday. The center of this storm moved from northern Missouri northeast across the Midweek and eastern Great Lakes region by late Tuesday night.||A large area of light to moderate precipitation developed across southern and central Minnesota early Tuesday morning and moved northeast while heavier precipitation started to develop across south central, and into east central Minnesota, and adjacent west-central Wisconsin Tuesday morning. This secondary area of higher reflectivity was in response to the deepening southern jet and associated upper level forcing. This led to moderate to occasional heavy snowfall across parts of west central Wisconsin. A band of 4 to 10 inches of snow fell from the southeastern Twin Cities metro area, northeast across west central Wisconsin from Red Wing, to Rice Lake and Ladysmith.||Some of the higher snowfall totals include:||9.5 inches near Baldwin.|8.0 inches near Boyceville. |7.7 inches south of River Falls. |7.5 inches near Wheeler. |7.5 inches in Roberts.|7.1 inches in River Falls.|7.0 inches in Chetek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began Tuesday morning, February 22nd. However, the bulk of the heavier snowfall, and higher snowfall rates, occurred during the afternoon hours. By the time the light snow tapered off, locally 6 to 8 inches fell across the county. Possibly locally higher snowfall amounts near the Rusk, and Sawyer county border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Eastern Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A wave of storms in the afternoon produced a long-track supercell with large hail and damaging wind over the Twin Cities metro and West Central Wisconsin. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon over the Twin Cities metro area, moved into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon and early evening. Several of the hailstones measured up to 2 inches in diameter and damaging winds occurred near Spring Valley and Elmwood.

A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern and east central Minnesota before moving into west central Wisconsin by the evening. By the time it moved into west central Wisconsin, a large bow echo had formed and produced damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blew down and blocked a highway.

By the time it moved into west central Wisconsin, a large bow echo had formed and produced damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blew down and landed on County Road L.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on Tuesday (5/10) across north-central and far northeast Wisconsin.  The strongest storms produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Baileys Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary tracked through the area along with a weak upper level disturbance combined with daytime heating to produce strong thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours on Friday (5/13). The strongest storms produced hail up to nickel size across portions of north-central and east-central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in Laona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary tracked through the area along with a weak upper level disturbance combined with daytime heating to produce strong thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours on Friday (5/13). The strongest storms produced hail up to nickel size across portions of north-central and east-central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell near Appleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid level shortwave combined with weak instability and strong shear to generate strong to severe storms across central and north-central Wisconsin during the evening hours on Wednesday (5/18).  The strongest storms produced wind damage and half dollar size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near Marshfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a stalled out warm front across western Wisconsin and tracked east through central Wisconsin during the early evening hours on Thursday (5/19).  The strongest storms produced isolated wind damage and hail up to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Birnamwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a stalled out warm front across western Wisconsin and tracked east through central Wisconsin during the early evening hours on Thursday (5/19).  The strongest storms produced isolated wind damage and hail up to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near Hamburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front. One report of large hail and another report of wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to one inch hail, wind gusts to 45 mph also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front. One report of large hail and another report of wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and branches down at the Wind Christmas Tree Farm. Some structural damage to the pole barns and other outbuildings, such as doors blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly large branches and some small sheds that were lofted from this weak tornado.  Storm spotters shared video of the tornado from a nearby field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lone tornado on this day with most of this damage being from broken tree parts and some small sheds that were lofted.  One trampoline was blown from the backyard over the top of the house and into the front yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of snow showers developed ahead of a strong upper trough tracking through the western Great Lakes region during Friday morning (4/15).  The snow showers intensified during the morning over north-central Wisconsin, then moved east along the Highway 29 corridor. The snow squall caused visibilities to fall to around one quarter of a mile with gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms moved across western Wisconsin on April 12th. The first round occurred in the afternoon as storms producing large hail moved across the area. Quarter to walnut sized hail fell from Galesville (Trempealeau County) east to New Rome (Adams County). The second round occurred in the evening as more severe storms moved through. These storms blew the roof off a barn near Tomah (Monroe County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Eastern Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A wave of storms in the afternoon produced a long-track supercell with large hail and damaging wind over the Twin Cities metro and West Central Wisconsin. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon over the Twin Cities metro area, moved into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon and early evening. Several of the hailstones measured up to 2 inches in diameter and damaging winds occurred near Spring Valley and Elmwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were over a dozen pine trees that were snapped off above the base, northwest of Weston. A few power poles were also broken. There were also unconfirmed reports of more trees and power lines down across southern Dunn County, but additional information was not available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved from northeastern South Dakota on the morning of the 5th to near the Twin Cities by the morning of the 6th, spreading precipitation across the Northland. Rain moved in during the daylight hours of the 5th before changing to a wintry mix by late evening and then all snow by Tuesday morning. Northeasterly winds off Lake Superior led to lake and terrain-enhancement along the South Shore on the 7th into the 8th as the low drifted into central Wisconsin. Snow gradually tapered off through the day on the 8th leaving 6 to 8 inches of snowfall near Lake Superior with totals quickly dropping off to the south. The Poplar area in Douglas County saw the most snow with this system at just short of 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far northern part of the county received 6 to 8 inches of snow through the event with totals quickly dropping to the south. A 3-day total of nearly 11 inches was reported near Poplar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across western Wisconsin during the evening of May 19th and produced a brief tornado, some damaging winds, and large hail. An EF0 tornado occurred southeast of Sparta (Monroe County) and destroyed a camper and outbuilding along with damaging farm equipment and trees. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse (La Crosse County) airport measured a 62 mph gust and a roof was damaged near Barre Mills (La Crosse County). Most of the hail that fell was quarter sized or smaller and the largest reported was golf ball sized in Holmen (La Crosse County). Around 3,800 customers were without power in La Crosse after the storms rolled through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport measured a 62 mph gust.

A dry-line/cold front that spawn a couple of tornadoes in eastern Minnesota, moved across west central Wisconsin during the evening and produced an additional three tornadoes, plus wind damage, and large hail. The tornadoes occurred near Lake Pepin, near Knapp, and between Colfax and Elk Mound in Dunn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began just north of I-94 near Rusk. The damage continued through Whitetail Golf Course. An irrigation system was tipped over, an outbuilding was torn apart, a trailer tipped over, and hundreds of trees were uprooted or snapped. Although the maximum wind speed was estimated at 90 mph, at times the winds were not as strong as 90 mph.

An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well with the largest stones being golf ball sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 59 mph was reported at the KASX ASOS at the Ashland Airport.

The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well with the largest stones being golf ball sized.

Severe storms moved across western Wisconsin during the evening of May 19th and produced a brief tornado, some damaging winds, and large hail. An EF0 tornado occurred southeast of Sparta (Monroe County) and destroyed a camper and outbuilding along with damaging farm equipment and trees. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse (La Crosse County) airport measured a 62 mph gust and a roof was damaged near Barre Mills (La Crosse County). Most of the hail that fell was quarter sized or smaller and the largest reported was golf ball sized in Holmen (La Crosse County). Around 3,800 customers were without power in La Crosse after the storms rolled through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell on on the south side of Holmen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that produced large hail across central and east central Minnesota during the morning and early afternoon hours of Thursday, May 12th, produced additional storms that afternoon over Rusk County. These storms produced large hail between Weyerhaeuser and Bruce.

A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern and east central Minnesota before moving into west central Wisconsin by the evening. By the time it moved into west central Wisconsin, a large bow echo had formed and produced damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near St. Croix Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swath of light to moderate snowfall occurred across southern WI due to the passage of an upper level disturbance and low pressure moving across the Ohio River Valley. Some schools were cancelled over south central WI. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred,  including one indirect death from a head-on collision in Green County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Eastern Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A wave of storms in the afternoon produced a long-track supercell with large hail and damaging wind over the Twin Cities metro and West Central Wisconsin. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon over the Twin Cities metro area, moved into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon and early evening. Several of the hailstones measured up to 2 inches in diameter and damaging winds occurred near Spring Valley and Elmwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo showed the lawn was mostly white with hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet then to snow as strong low pressure tracked from eastern Illinois to western Lake Huron. The burst of snow occurred during the morning rush hour and the slick roads resulted in many vehicle slide-offs and accidents, especially on secondary roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick 1-2 inches during the morning rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved from northeastern South Dakota on the morning of the 5th to near the Twin Cities by the morning of the 6th, spreading precipitation across the Northland. Rain moved in during the daylight hours of the 5th before changing to a wintry mix by late evening and then all snow by Tuesday morning. Northeasterly winds off Lake Superior led to lake and terrain-enhancement along the South Shore on the 7th into the 8th as the low drifted into central Wisconsin. Snow gradually tapered off through the day on the 8th leaving 6 to 8 inches of snowfall near Lake Superior with totals quickly dropping off to the south. The Poplar area in Douglas County saw the most snow with this system at just short of 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 6 inches in the southern part of the county to 6 to 8 inches across the Bayfield Peninsula. One station near the top of the terrain ridge received a total of 8.2 inches from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains on the 12th to over Minnesota on the morning of the 14th and became occluded. A cold front pushed across northwest Wisconsin on the 13th with heavy rainfall being received in some locations. Rainfall totals or 2-3 inches were recorded around Washburn County with most falling in a 5 hour period. Additionally, frost was still in the ground leading to higher than normal runoff rates. This lead to isolated minor flooding of ditches along with a few shoulder and road washouts in the Washburn County area. The worst damage was two large culvert pipes that were washed out in Barronett causing damage to a road as well. Additionally, a school bus in Price County tried to drive through a flooded roadway and became stuck as the shoulder gave way underneath it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school bus became stranded in flood waters when the shoulder washed out underneath it and it partially tipped. Four students and the driver were helped from the bus to safety and there were no injuries reported, although the bus driver was taken to the hospital to be checked out. The bus went through the flooded section of road 15 minutes prior to returning and trying to make it through a second time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains on the 12th to over Minnesota on the morning of the 14th and became occluded. A cold front pushed across northwest Wisconsin on the 13th with heavy rainfall being received in some locations. Rainfall totals or 2-3 inches were recorded around Washburn County with most falling in a 5 hour period. Additionally, frost was still in the ground leading to higher than normal runoff rates. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spring storm coupled with frost leaving the ground led to excessive runoff that resulted in damage to two culverts. Pictures show culverts being pushed upward. Each culvert is 84 inches by 70 feet with preliminary estimated costs of $20,000 to replace culverts and make repairs to the road along with time and equipment. The Town of Baronett requested an emergency declaration to help cover the costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm tracked across central and north-central Wisconsin during the early morning hours on Thursday (5/12) north of a warm front and associated with a mesoscale convective vortex.  The strongest storm produced large hail up to quarter size as it tracked through central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Schofield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along an instability ribbon from central to northeast Wisconsin during the afternoon hours on Thursday (5/12).  The strongest storms produced large hail across this area.  Further north, an outflow boundary caused wind damage across portions of north-central Wisconsin. Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rain dumped upwards of 7 inches of rain across central Oconto County and southern Marinette County during the late afternoon and evening hours, causing $465,500 worth of damage to area roads and bridges across these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Mosinee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from northwestern Iowa to northern Lake Michigan on March 5th, spreading an area of freezing rain and snow across much of northwestern Wisconsin ahead of a warm front. Freezing rain moved in during the early morning hours of the 5th and lingered into the evening before transitioning over to snow overnight and tapering off the morning of the 6th. Ice accumulations of 0.25 to nearly 0.40 were reported across northwest Wisconsin with the highest report being 0.38 near Moquah, most of which fell in around six hours. Additionally, snowfall of up to 5 to 7 inches fell during the latter half of the storm with 7.5 accumulating near Washburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 5 followed about a hundreth of an inch of ice accumulation early in the storm near Solon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking northeast from the southern Plains to the Great Lakes brought a swath of snow and ice to northern Wisconsin from Tuesday evening (3/22) through midday on Wednesday (3/23). Snowfall amounts were generally 4 to 8 inches across portions of north-central Wisconsin along with 2 to 3 tenths of an inch of ice from freezing rain.  The combination of snow and ice caused significant damage to trees and power lines, with over 50,000 customers without power in northern Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 7.6 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 6.3 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), and 5.9 inches in St. Germain (Vilas Co.).  Some of the highest ice totals included 0.3 inches in Manitowish Waters (Vilas Co.), Argonne (Forest Co.), as well as southeast of Niagara (northern Marinette Co.) and 0.2 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), as well as in St. Germain (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 7.6 inches in Eagle River, 5.9 inches in St. Germain, and 5.2 inches southwest of Land O' Lakes.  The highest ice totals across Vilas County included 0.3 inches in Manitowish Waters and 0.2 inches in St. Germain as well as southwest of Land O' Lakes.

The combination of snow and ice caused significant damage to trees and power lines, with over 50,000 customers without power in northern Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 7.6 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 6.3 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), and 5.9 inches in St. Germain (Vilas Co.).  Some of the highest ice totals included 0.3 inches in Manitowish Waters (Vilas Co.), Argonne (Forest Co.), as well as southeast of Niagara (northern Marinette Co.) and 0.2 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), as well as in St. Germain (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Oneida County included 6.3 inches in Rhinelander and 5.5 inches in Hazelhurst.  The highest ice total across Oneida County included 0.2 inches in Rhinelander.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across Forest County included 4.0 inches northwest of Argonne.  The highest ice totals across Forest County included 0.3 inches in Argonne and 0.2 inches northwest of Argonne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from northwestern Iowa to northern Lake Michigan on March 5th, spreading an area of freezing rain and snow across much of northwestern Wisconsin ahead of a warm front. Freezing rain moved in during the early morning hours of the 5th and lingered into the evening before transitioning over to snow overnight and tapering off the morning of the 6th. Ice accumulations of 0.25 to nearly 0.40 were reported across northwest Wisconsin with the highest report being 0.38 near Moquah, most of which fell in around six hours. Additionally, snowfall of up to 5 to 7 inches fell during the latter half of the storm with 7.5 accumulating near Washburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were reported in a time period of around six to eight hours in the Hayward area at the beginning of the storm. Jump River Electrical Cooperative reported approximately 500 customers without power during the height of the storm due to downed lines from the ice and trees/branches falling onto lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff after a rainfall event created some western Wisconsin river flooding in late March. Up to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the area and helped to cause flooding along the Black and Yellow Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff after a rainfall event pushed the Black River out of its banks in Black River Falls. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 48.36 feet on the 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff after a rainfall event pushed the Black River out of its banks near Galesville. The river crested almost a half foot above the flood stage at 12.44 feet on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster/line of thunderstorms moved across southern Wisconsin the evening of March 5th.  Within this line of storms, some briefly intense downburst winds occurred across portions of south central to east central Wisconsin.  A brief tornado also occurred in southeast Dane county.  Quite a bit of structural damage was noted to a number of farming sheds and buildings in areas of Dane, Rock and Fond du Lac counties. Heavy rainfall on a frozen ground also led to some basement flooding and minor street flooding.|A home was struck by lightning in the Town of Deerfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds removed small portions of the roof decking and shingles from the church and school buildings. Some of the siding and gutters were removed from the school. A home sustained minor damage to the siding, shingles, and screened-in porch. Some windows were broken in several of the structures. Several trees or tree limbs were downed in the area. A dumpster was pushed and rolled across a parking lot. A trailer was also thrown and damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some river flooding occurred across western Wisconsin in early March following a rainfall event. Up to 1.5 inches of rain fell and with the ground still frozen, the increased runoff caused flooding to occur along the Trempealeau River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the area in early March. With the ground still frozen, the resulting runoff pushed the Trempealeau River out of its banks in Dodge. The river crested over a half foot above the flood stage at 11.09 feet on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some river flooding occurred across western Wisconsin in early March following a rainfall event. Up to 1.5 inches of rain fell and with the ground still frozen, the increased runoff caused flooding to occur along the Trempealeau River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the area in early March. With the ground still frozen, the resulting runoff pushed the Trempealeau River out of its banks in Arcadia. The river crested just over the flood stage at 8.15 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking well south of Wisconsin brought a band of heavier snow to portions of east-central Wisconsin during the early morning hours on Monday (3/7).  Snowfall totals were generally 4 to 7 inches across Manitowoc, Calumet, and Winnebago counties.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 7.0 inches in Osman (Manitowoc Co.), 6.3 inches near Saint Nazianz (Manitowoc Co.), and 5.0 inches near Chilton as well as near Oshkosh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Manitowoc County included 7.0 inches in Osman and 6.3 inches near Saint Nazianz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a round of heavy snow to a small portion of southwest Wisconsin from the evening of March 6th into the morning of the 7th. Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches were common across Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across Grant County. The highest reported total was 7 inches near Platteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure moved from the Dakotas early on the 29th to central Iowa the morning of the 30th. This low track spread a mix of rain, snow and wintry mix across much of the Northland. While most of northwestern Wisconsin received sub-warnings conditions during the storm, heavier freezing rain was reported across parts of Bayfield, Sawyer and Price counties were 0.25 to almost 0.40 inches of accumulation was received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy freezing rain was recorded across parts of Sawyer County with amounts around a quarter of an inch or higher in many areas. The town of Ojibwa received 0.38 inches of ice accumulation by the time freezing rain ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure moved from the Dakotas early on the 29th to central Iowa the morning of the 30th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain decreased from west to east across the county with more rain being received in eastern areas. Around a quarter of an inch of ice was received along the Price/Sawyer County line with the system.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavier than expected freezing rain was received across the Bayfield Peninsula during the storm due to onshore winds from Lake Superior bringing cooler surface temperatures into the area. A quarter of an inch of ice was recorded near Red Cliff with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small wildfire in the median of Interstate 94 near Northfield (Jackson County) contributed to a two vehicle crash. Smoke from the fire created low visibility in the southbound lanes where two vehicles collided. No injuries were reported from the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smoke from a small wildfire in the median of Interstate 94 near Northfield contributed to a two vehicle crash. The smoke caused low visibilities to occur in the southbound lanes of the Interstate where the crash occurred. No injuries occurred as a result of the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving out of the Central Plains brought a warm front through Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin during the morning hours. Supported by a strong low level jet and warm air pushing into the region, storms spread north through the morning hours. The first round of storms mainly produced hail with the largest being tennis ball sized. A cold front associated with the low then moved through during the afternoon leading to another round of storms that produced hail to the size of quarters. Insurance companies estimated $10 million in property and automobile claims from the storms in Washburn County alone.

A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The northwest Wisconsin area mainly saw impacts with the first round of storms with hail to 2 inches in diameter and numerous reports of trees down in the Spooner and Hayward areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many tree branches down at the entrance of a subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main severe storm that moved across portions of southern and central Minnesota, then moved across west-central Wisconsin and produced large hail, up to ping pong size, before weakening in northern Wisconsin. Strong winds damaged a number of farms.  There was a brief tornado north of Saint Croix Falls, which damaged three farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two farms sustained significant damage to outbuildings, with the tin roofs partially removed from machine sheds and one small shed collapsed. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main severe storm that moved across portions of southern and central Minnesota, then moved across west-central Wisconsin and produced large hail, up to ping pong size, before weakening in northern Wisconsin. Strong winds damaged a number of farms.  There was a brief tornado north of Saint Croix Falls, which damaged three farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several dozen trees were toppled over several blocks north of downtown St Croix Falls. Some fell on houses and vehicles. All trees and large limbs were downed toward the west and west-northwest. Time estimated based on radar.

A warm front moving into the region during the afternoon hours of the 11th led to the development of severe thunderstorms across the area. As these storms moved across northwest Wisconsin during the evening hours, they produced small hail along with tree damage in the Spooner and Hayward areas before weakening and exiting the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 12 inch diameter poplar trees were snapped during the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted east of Cornell.

A significant event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Eastern Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A wave of storms in the afternoon produced a long-track supercell with large hail and damaging wind over the Twin Cities metro and West Central Wisconsin. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon over the Twin Cities metro area, moved into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon and early evening. Several of the hailstones measured up to 2 inches in diameter and damaging winds occurred near Spring Valley and Elmwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground was mostly white with hail.

Although the storms were not as severe as when they were in Minnesota, there was a report of wind damage near Chippewa Falls, and a measured wind gust of 58 MPH took place at the Menomonie airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down in North Eau Claire. Other trees of various sizes also blew down on the north side of Eau Claire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north-northeasterly winds across western Lake Superior led to waves of 2 to 4 feet along the eastern facing shores of Madeline Island. A man cliff jumping at the cliffs in Big Bay State Park on the southeast corner of the island was overcome by the surf and drowned. Additionally, a man fishing on Chequamegon Bay was last seen during the afternoon hours with his body not being found until later that week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2:38PM CDT on the afternoon of the 7th, La Pointe Police, La Pointe Fire and Madeline Island EMS were dispatched to Big Bay State Park at the southeast corner of the island for a man that was cliff jumping at the Eagle's Nest cliffs and did not resurface. The man had completed one jump and on the second, succumbed to the rough surf conditions in place (41, M). He was alone in the water at the time. Winds were from the NNE at around 10 mph with gusts to around 20 mph at the time with waves around 2 to 4 feet. A Small Craft Advisory was in effect at the time. The man's body was not recovered until the early afternoon on the 8th. Multiple agencies assisted with the search and recovery efforts including the Wisconsin DNR, US Park Service, USCG, Bayfield Fire Dive Team, Sawyer County Dive Team, Sawyer County Search and Rescue, Ashland County Sheriffs Office, Great Lakes Indian Fish & Wildlife Commission and Mashkiiziibii Conservation Wardens from the Bad River Tribe. In a separate incident just east of Ashland on Chequamegon Bay, a 60 year old man was last seen fishing during the afternoon hours alone amid the large waves and windy conditions. His empty boat was found washed ashore the next morning (Monday) near Second Landing Water Access and he was reported missing when he failed to report for work that morning. His body was located about a half-mile from where his boat washed ashore Thursday night (60, M). While a Small Craft Advisory was in effect on Lake Superior, one was not in effect for the waters of Chequamegon Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster/line of thunderstorms moved across southern Wisconsin the evening of March 5th.  Within this line of storms, some briefly intense downburst winds occurred across portions of south central to east central Wisconsin.  A brief tornado also occurred in southeast Dane county.  Quite a bit of structural damage was noted to a number of farming sheds and buildings in areas of Dane, Rock and Fond du Lac counties. Heavy rainfall on a frozen ground also led to some basement flooding and minor street flooding.|A home was struck by lightning in the Town of Deerfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a residential home, causing a fire that resulted in significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boat capsized in rough water conditions off Iron County the morning of the 25th. Of the two occupants in the boat, one had already passed by the time rescuers reached the capsized boat off Marble Point. It took additional time for the rescuers to reach the men due to the remoteness of the location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were boating on Lake Superior off Iron County during the morning hours of Thursday the 25th. Northeast winds of 10 to 15 knots with gusts to 25 knots were reported during the early morning hours with waves of 3 to 5 feet. A Small Craft Advisory was in effect. Iron County Sheriff's Office received a 911 call around 8:55AM CDT that morning from one of the men in the boat reporting that the boat had capsized near Marble Point. Due to the water conditions and lack of road access to the Marble Point area, it took rescuers additional time to reach the two men. One of the men (64, M) had died and rescuers brought the other man to shore where he declined medical attention. The rescue process lasted until just after 4PM CDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused some rock slides to occur neat Potosi. A few culverts became clogged with debris sending water over State Highway 133.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry-line/cold front that spawn a couple of tornadoes in eastern Minnesota, moved across west central Wisconsin during the evening and produced an additional three tornadoes, plus wind damage, and large hail. The tornadoes occurred near Lake Pepin, near Knapp, and between Colfax and Elk Mound in Dunn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The starting point was estimated based on video evidence as it began over the Mississippi River (Lake Pepin). Once onshore, the tornado knocked down or broke about one dozen trees as it moved up the river bluffs and dissipated. Maximum winds were estimated at 70 mph.

A river outlet current from the Pike River pulled two children in the Lake Michigan. One child was able to safely make it back to shore on their own, while one child drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5-year old boy swimming at Pennoyer Park in Kenosha got caught in a river outlet current from the Pike River and drowned in Lake Michigan. An 8-year old girl was also struggling in the current, but was able to make it back to shore. She was transported to a local hospital with non life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across parts of northwestern Wisconsin at the start of November. A few areas of D2 Severe Drought were located near Chequamegon Bay and northern Douglas County at the beginning of the month, but a heavy rainfall event during the second week of the month led to rapid drought improvement by the middle of the month. All areas of D2 Severe Drought were removed with the update on the 15th with the area seeing well above normal precipitation over the course of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought across most of the northern half of the county. A heavy rain event was received the second week of the month and led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 15th. Precipitation for the month ended up around 1 inch above normal with D1 Moderate Drought persisting for the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin on July 23rd. The first occurred in the afternoon and produced a tornado and wind damage. A brief EF0 tornado occurred just east of La Crosse (La Crosse County) that damaged trees along its path. The storms also produced high winds that caused considerable damage in and near Stoddard (Vernon County). A camper was blown over injuring the occupants and numerous trees and power lines were blown down. Trees were also blown down from near Viroqua (Vernon County) east to Ontario (Vernon County) and La Farge (Vernon County). The second round of storms occurred in the late evening and blew down trees near Wauzeka (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down north of Stoddard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary across central Minnesota, and an increasing very moist airmass with PWATs near 1.75 early Friday morning, July 15th, was the setup for a flash flood event over Chippewa County Wisconsin. During the early morning, several waves of thunderstorms developed across west central, Wisconsin, and east central Minnesota, and drifted southeast across west central Wisconsin for several hours. The upper level flow was conducive for training of the thunderstorms as a strong low level jet at 850H impinged upon very moist airmass. By daybreak, a pocket of 4 to 5 inches of rain fell in Chippewa County where flash flooding was reported. Elsewhere, rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches from Barron to Chippewa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local authorities reported closed roads due to flooding in the city of Stanley. In addition, several culverts were washed out and streams were running high between Stanley and Colburn. Based on the flash flood guidance, the rainfall totals were nearly two times that amount to flood. Radar estimates between Colburn and Stanley average between 4 and 6 inches, with an unofficial report of 5.40 inches in the city of Stanley.

Ample wind shear of 30-40 knots, combined with 1500 J/kg of CAPE, caused a few storms to develop across west central Wisconsin. Some hail and gust winds occurred with the strongest storms, one of which caused a wind gust of 67 mph at the KEAU airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms with heavy rain moved across north-central Wisconsin during the early morning of July 15th. Radar estimates indicate a band of 3 to 5 inches of rain fell from northwest Clark County to the southeast part of the county. This heavy rain caused water to go over a few county roads near Chili (Clark County) and Thorp (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed some creeks out of their banks. Water from the South Branch O'Neill Creek went over County Highway Y between Chili and U.S. Highway 10. Water from the North Fork Eau Claire River went over County Highway X west of Thorp, and County Highway H northwest of Chili was covered by standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system tracking across the Great Lakes produced strong winds to 60 mph across the Fox Valley during the afternoon on Saturday (11/5). Peak winds of 60 mph were measured in Forest Junction (Calumet Co.) and Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds of 60 mph were measured at the Oshkosh airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a Colorado low lifted to the northeast on the 11th into the 12th, high pressure built across the Central and Northern Plains. This set up a tight pressure gradient over western Lake Superior and generated strong northerly winds that ushered in cooler air. With Lake Superior water temperatures still in the 40s, this generated a long duration lake-effect snow event over parts of northwestern Wisconsin. The primary area where snow was received was the snowbelt over northern Iron County. By the time the event ended after about 48 hours, snowfall totals nearing 14 inches were reported in Gile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake-effect snowfall across the Iron County snowbelt was observed over a two-day period on the 12th and 13th. Total snowfall was 13.8 inches in Gile with about 7.5 falling in an 8-hour period the morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across northern Wisconsin and Upper Michigan the 17th into the 18th. This track led to northerly winds across western Lake Superior leading to lake-effect snowfall across northwestern Wisconsin. The bands of snow first affected northern Douglas County before pivoting eastward as winds shifted from northeasterly to northerly. The band then stalled out over Iron County and dumped upwards of 2 feet of snow on the Gile area before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake-effect snow totals of 6 to 9 inches were recorded across the northern part of Douglas County. The highest reports 9 inches near Superior and Waino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving area of low pressure moved from eastern Kansas to north of Lake Superior along a cold front the 29th into the 30th. This brought a quick, heavy burst of snowfall to northwestern Wisconsin during the day on the 29th. Snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches were common across the region in roughly 12 hours. A few spots received slightly higher amounts to around 9 to 10 inches during the event near Butternut, Lake Nebagamon and near Gordon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick moving system brought heavy snow to the county over a period of about 12 hours with snowfall totals of 3 to 7 inches from south to north. Higher amounts to just under 10 inches were reported 13 miles east-northeast of Gordon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary across central Minnesota, and an increasing very moist airmass with PWATs near 1.75 early Friday morning, July 15th, was the setup for a flash flood event over Chippewa County Wisconsin. During the early morning, several waves of thunderstorms developed across west central, Wisconsin, and east central Minnesota, and drifted southeast across west central Wisconsin for several hours. The upper level flow was conducive for training of the thunderstorms as a strong low level jet at 850H impinged upon very moist airmass. By daybreak, a pocket of 4 to 5 inches of rain fell in Chippewa County where flash flooding was reported. Elsewhere, rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches from Barron to Chippewa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5 hour rainfall report of 3.36 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across parts of northwestern Wisconsin at the start of November. A few areas of D2 Severe Drought were located near Chequamegon Bay and northern Douglas County at the beginning of the month, but a heavy rainfall event during the second week of the month led to rapid drought improvement by the middle of the month. All areas of D2 Severe Drought were removed with the update on the 15th with the area seeing well above normal precipitation over the course of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought across the far northeastern corner of the county, primarily in the Ashland area. A heavy rain event was received the second week of the month and led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 15th. Precipitation for the month ended up around 1 to 3 inches above normal with D1 Moderate Drought persisting for the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin on July 23rd. The first occurred in the afternoon and produced a tornado and wind damage. A brief EF0 tornado occurred just east of La Crosse (La Crosse County) that damaged trees along its path. The storms also produced high winds that caused considerable damage in and near Stoddard (Vernon County). A camper was blown over injuring the occupants and numerous trees and power lines were blown down. Trees were also blown down from near Viroqua (Vernon County) east to Ontario (Vernon County) and La Farge (Vernon County). The second round of storms occurred in the late evening and blew down trees near Wauzeka (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down across and near La Crosse. Some of the downed trees caused damage to homes and buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow fell from southwest Minnesota through the Twin Cities metro and up through northwest Wisconsin on Tuesday, November 29, 2022. This band was caused by strong frontogenesis within the 800 to 900 mb layer. ||Snowfall rates within this band were over an inch per hour. Total snowfall amounts within this band ranged from 4 to 8 inches. ||The impacts were typical for a winter storm. Schools closed early, and there were many traffic accidents, including jackknifed semis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across all of Barron County early morning hours Tuesday into Tuesday evening, where several sources reported 4-7 inches of snowfall. The highest amount was recorded near Rice Lake, WI, with 7 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms that developed between the northern Twin Cities metro area, and  Amery in southern Polk County over west-central Wisconsin, quickly became severe with large hailstones falling across the area. The worst area that received up to 3 inch diameter hail occurred around River Falls. This storm tracked between Hudson and River Falls, southeast before moving into Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties. Although there wasn't any other reports of hail southeast of River Falls, based on radar and the track of the severe storm, very large hail likely fell between River Falls, Spring Valley, Eau Galle, Durand and Ellsworth. Other storms that became severe occurred between Menomonie, Eau Claire and Ladysmith, Wisconsin. There were pictures of cars damaged from the hailstone in River Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed Friday afternoon along a cold front that was moving southeast across west-central Wisconsin. Ample instability and marginal wind shear allowed for one storm to produced 1.5 inch hail east of Deer Park, Wisconsin, in the far northeast corner of St. Croix County. Most of the other storms that late afternoon and early evening weren't as strong and may have produced some hail and gusty winds, but no other reports were received.

An unusually slow moving low pressure system with abundant moisture produced 5-9 inches of rain in a 24 hour period from early morning on September 11th to early morning on September 12th. Numerous roads and hundreds of basements experienced flooding in portions of Waukesha, Milwaukee, and Racine Counties. Some rivers and creeks also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Root River in southwest Milwaukee County reached minor flood stage with portions of the Root River Parkway and Oakwood Road flooded and closed. Oak Creek at South Milwaukee also reached minor flood stage causing flooding in backyards. There was flooding of lowlands and agricultural fields. Many basements were also flooded. The 1st floor of a retail store flooded due to a leaking roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front and strong upper level disturbance brought a line of strong to severe thunderstorms across southern WI for the late afternoon and early evening hours. Tree damage from straight-line winds was the most prevalent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of trees and branches down, including on roadways. A 5 foot diameter tree was uprooted and fallen onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms that developed between the northern Twin Cities metro area, and  Amery in southern Polk County over west-central Wisconsin, quickly became severe with large hailstones falling across the area. Other storms that became severe occurred between Menomonie, Eau Claire and Ladysmith, Wisconsin. There were pictures of cars damaged from the hailstone in River Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media had several pictures of large hail that fell around River Falls, Wisconsin. One of the pictures measured 3 inches in diameter. Based on pictures and social media posts, there was a swath of this large hail that occurred on the northwest side of River Falls, and moved over the town, and exited on the southeast side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually slow moving low pressure system with abundant moisture produced 5-9 inches of rain in a 24 hour period from early morning on September 11th to early morning on September 12th. Numerous roads and hundreds of basements experienced flooding in portions of Waukesha, Milwaukee, and Racine Counties. Some rivers and creeks also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads and intersections with standing water were closed, including I-94 at Highway T. Both lanes of this portion of the interstate were closed for the morning into the early afternoon of September 12th. Numerous basements were also flooded.  The Fox River at Waukesha also rose into minor flood stage. Portions of Frame Park become flooded from the river. About one half mile south of downtown Waukesha, water was covering much of the back yards of 6 or 7 homes in the Bethesda Court area of Waukesha due to the flooding river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water on roads across the county including in the city of Racine.  There was flooding of lowlands and agricultural fields. Hundreds of basements were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple months of near to below normal rainfall along with sandy soils in the region led to widespread D0 and D1 drought conditions in the tier of counties along the South Shore of Lake Superior. After a heavy rain event over part of the area early in the month, conditions trended drier for the remainder of the month and led to the introduction of a few areas of D2 Severe Drought across parts of Ashland, Bayfield and Douglas counties during the middle of the month and persisted into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After spending just over the first half of the month either in D0 Abnormally Dry or D1 Moderate Drought, dry conditions from around the middle of the month onward led to the introduction of an area of D2 Severe Drought in the Ashland area and areas immediately adjacent to Chequamegon Bay on the 20th. Total rainfall for the month of September ended up normal with most of the monthly total coming in one day when 2 inches were recorded at the Ashland Airport on the 9th. However, this was not enough to prevent the area of D2 Severe Drought from being introduced a week later and then persisting into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in cyclonic northwest flow during the afternoon hours on Sunday (9/25).  The storms produced sub-severe wind gusts and hail across northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in Lake Tomahawk. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

However, this was not enough to prevent the area of D2 Severe Drought from being introduced a week later and then persisting into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the Northland produced showers and storms across the region during the evening hours of the 2nd. While most of the storms remained below severe limits, one storm did produce 1 inch diameter hail near the town of Winter in Sawyer County.

An approaching low pressure area and arctic front brought light to moderate snow, brisk winds, and blowing and drifting snow to southern WI. Snow covered roads, especially on north to south roads and secondary roads, resulted in some vehicle slide-offs and accidents. The biggest impacts occurred over south central WI where greater snow accumulation had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 3-5 inches. Some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and deepening low pressure from the Lower Great Lakes into southern Quebec brought an arctic air mass with wind gusts of 30-50 mph across southern WI. Minimum wind chill temperatures ranged from 35 below to 45 below zero. Some blowing and drifting snow occurred during this time. Schools and many public and private events were cancelled. Warming centers were opened especially in large urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranging from 30 below to 41 below zero.
