EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system across the central Plains tracked northeast towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday (12/21).  The low tracked through the western Great Lakes during the morning hours on Thursday (12/22), bringing a cold front through Wisconsin. The low deepened over central Lake Superior on Thursday (12/22), then continued deepening as it tracked across the central Great Lakes Thursday night (12/22) and western Quebec Friday afternoon (12/23). The low continued to deepen as it slowly drifted north towards James Bay on Saturday (12/24).||An initial band of snow developed across northeast Wisconsin Wednesday afternoon (12/21) through Thursday morning (12/22) with 3 to 6 inches across the region.  There was a lull in the light snow on Thursday (12/22) as temperatures dropped precipitously behind the cold front and west winds increased throughout the day.||Winds increased to 30 to 50 mph Thursday night (12/22) into Friday (12/23) with considerable blowing and drifting snow as well as additional light snow.  Wind chills dropped to 25 to 45 below zero during this period, with the coldest values along and west of the Fox Valley.  Two-day snowfall totals by Friday had increased to 4 to 8 inches across much of the area, with up to 10 inches in northern Door County.||High winds, blowing and drifting snow, and cold wind chills continued into Saturday (12/24).  Winds of 30 to 50 mph continued into Saturday (12/24) with wind chills of 15 to 25 below zero.  While the heaviest snow had ended across the region by Saturday (12/24), additional lake effect snow continued into Saturday morning; boosting three-day totals up to 12 to 15 inches in the lake effect snowbelt region of Vilas County.||The highest three-day snowfall totals were 15.4 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.) and 12.2 inches in Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.) ending Saturday morning (12/24).  The highest two-day snowfall totals were 10.8 inches near Egg Harbor (Door Co.) and 9.1 inches in Washington Island (Door Co.) ending Friday morning (12/23).  The highest one-day snowfall total was 9 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.) ending Saturday morning (12/24).  The highest wind speed recorded during the event was 52 mph at Gills Rock (Door Co.) on Saturday morning (12/24).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures fell to -10 to -15 degrees during the morning of December 23rd. The combination of the bitter cold and gusty westerly winds created wind chills of -35 to -38 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought several inches of accumulating snow to much of southern WI. The snow began during the morning rush hour and lasted into the early afternoon. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and deepening low pressure from the Lower Great Lakes into southern Quebec brought an arctic air mass with wind gusts of 30-50 mph across southern WI. Minimum wind chill temperatures ranged from 35 below to 45 below zero. Some blowing and drifting snow occurred during this time. Schools and many public and private events were cancelled. Warming centers were opened especially in large urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranging from 30 below to 38 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Great Lakes, spreading a wintry mix of snow, freezing rain and sleet across the region on Wednesday (12/14). The mixed precipitation changed over to all snow over much of northern and central Wisconsin Wednesday evening (12/14) and across east-central Wisconsin early Thursday morning (12/15).  Once the changeover occurred, periods of heavy snow fell through Thursday morning (12/15) with east winds gusting to 20 to 30 mph at times. Snowfall amounts were generally 4 to 9 inches from the Fox Valley west to central and north-central Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 9.5 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 8.9 inches near Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.),  8.5 inches in Spencer (Marathon Co.), 8 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), near Woodruff and Minocqua (Oneida Co.), as well as near Colby and Weston (Marathon Co.), and 7.5 inches in Wausau and near Mosinee (Marathon Co.), near Polonia and near Amherst (Portage Co.), as well as near Goodnow (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 7 inches in Arbor Vitae and 6.2 inches near St. Germain.

The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chills to fall to -20 to -30 degrees Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system across the central Plains tracked northeast towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday (12/21).  The low tracked through the western Great Lakes during the morning hours on Thursday (12/22), bringing a cold front through Wisconsin. The low deepened over central Lake Superior on Thursday (12/22), then continued deepening as it tracked across the central Great Lakes Thursday night (12/22) and western Quebec Friday afternoon (12/23). The low continued to deepen as it slowly drifted north towards James Bay on Saturday (12/24).||An initial band of snow developed across northeast Wisconsin Wednesday afternoon (12/21) through Thursday morning (12/22) with 3 to 6 inches across the region.  There was a lull in the light snow on Thursday (12/22) as temperatures dropped precipitously behind the cold front and west winds increased throughout the day.||Winds increased to 30 to 50 mph Thursday night (12/22) into Friday (12/23) with considerable blowing and drifting snow as well as additional light snow.  Wind chills dropped to 25 to 45 below zero during this period, with the coldest values along and west of the Fox Valley.  Two-day snowfall totals by Friday had increased to 4 to 8 inches across much of the area, with up to 10 inches in northern Door County.||High winds, blowing and drifting snow, and cold wind chills continued into Saturday (12/24).  Winds of 30 to 50 mph continued into Saturday (12/24) with wind chills of 15 to 25 below zero.  While the heaviest snow had ended across the region by Saturday (12/24), additional lake effect snow continued into Saturday morning; boosting three-day totals up to 12 to 15 inches in the lake effect snowbelt region of Vilas County.||The highest three-day snowfall totals were 15.4 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.) and 12.2 inches in Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.) ending Saturday morning (12/24).  The highest two-day snowfall totals were 10.8 inches near Egg Harbor (Door Co.) and 9.1 inches in Washington Island (Door Co.) ending Friday morning (12/23).  The highest one-day snowfall total was 9 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.) ending Saturday morning (12/24).  The highest wind speed recorded during the event was 52 mph at Gills Rock (Door Co.) on Saturday morning (12/24).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Great Lakes, spreading a wintry mix of snow, freezing rain and sleet across the region on Wednesday (12/14). The mixed precipitation changed over to all snow over much of northern and central Wisconsin Wednesday evening (12/14) and across east-central Wisconsin early Thursday morning (12/15).  Once the changeover occurred, periods of heavy snow fell through Thursday morning (12/15) with east winds gusting to 20 to 30 mph at times. Snowfall amounts were generally 4 to 9 inches from the Fox Valley west to central and north-central Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 9.5 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 8.9 inches near Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.),  8.5 inches in Spencer (Marathon Co.), 8 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), near Woodruff and Minocqua (Oneida Co.), as well as near Colby and Weston (Marathon Co.), and 7.5 inches in Wausau and near Mosinee (Marathon Co.), near Polonia and near Amherst (Portage Co.), as well as near Goodnow (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Florence County included 9.5 inches in Spread Eagle and 8 inches in Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of far northwestern Wisconsin on December 16, 2022. A slow moving low pressure system was in place across eastern Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin. Low level instability and moisture supported cellular snow squall development from the late morning through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibilities quickly dropped to around 1/4 mile or less as the squall passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossing the region during the overnight hours of the 20th into the 21st brought a band of showers and storms to northwest Wisconsin. A low level jet ahead of the front led to the storms becoming more intense as they moved across the area. These storms produced damaging winds that resulted in sporadic reports of downed trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were completely uprooted with many others snapped in half. A cement block shed had also shifted some due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the late morning and afternoon of June 15th. The morning storms produced some hail and wind damage. Quarter sized hail fell in Stoddard (Vernon County) and trees were blown down southeast of La Crosse (La Crosse County). The afternoon storms were stronger and produced three tornadoes along with wind damage. An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah and moved northeast before crossing into Juneau County north of Shennington. The tornado crossed Interstates 90 & 94 and passed just to the east of Wyeville. Along its path, extensive tree damage occurred with some downed trees landing on homes, numerous semis were tipped over along  Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries, a metal farm shed near Oakdale had several pieces of metal removed, and a few structures near Wyeville sustained damage, including at least one farm outbuilding that was destroyed. Up to three hour delays occurred along Interstate 90 after the tornado moved through. Monroe County declared a state of emergency.

An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado developed just to the southwest of Mauston and tracked northeast before dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Lake Dam. In Mauston, the tornado caused roof damage to the hospital and a restaurant, blew windows out of several vehicles near the hospital, took down several stop lights, blew over parked semis, snapped power poles and downed several trees in a mobile home park that damaged numerous homes. In Veteran's Memorial Park, stands for the baseball field were overturned with some blown across the street and the scoreboard was ripped off its posts. A tree fell on the roof of an apartment complex damaging the roof and allowing water to leak into some units. Northeast of Mauston over rural parts of Juneau County, the damage was mainly limited to trees. The mayor of Mauston issued a disaster declaration.

The afternoon storms were stronger and produced three tornadoes along with wind damage. An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado developed east of La Farge and moved northeast along State Highway 82 before dissipating north of White City. A house, farm outbuildings and a couple of campers were damaged along with numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a weak cold front across southern WI. A couple powerful and slow moving storms affected the Oconomowoc area, producing widespread golf ball sized hail. The hail damage was generally between Concord Road on the west, Highway 67 on the east, just north of Lac La Belle for the northern edge, and I-94 for the southern boundary. The hail lasted for 20-30 minutes and accumulated. Widespread damage to residential homes, businesses, vehicles, and agriculture occurred. More specifically, shingle, siding, downspout, and window screen damage. Some broken windows occurred. Gardens were shredded and trees were stripped of much of their leaves. The hail accumulated and blocked storm sewer runoff, resulting in street flooding. Some basement and business flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon across western Wisconsin and tracked through northeast Wisconsin through the evening hours on Wednesday (6/15).  The storms formed into a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) with multiple reports of damaging winds of 70 to 80 mph as well as 10 tornadoes embedded within the main line of thunderstorms.  The 10 tornadoes ties a record for the most tornadoes in an event for northeast Wisconsin.  Additional storms late in the evening produced a few reports of large hail near the lakeshore as they moved offshore.  ||The swath of damaging winds caused multiple power outages, with over 125,000 customers without power across northeast Wisconsin.  Power was not restored for several days across many areas as the cleanup from the storms took an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed at 6:34 pm CDT near Middle Inlet in central Marinette County and tracked northeast before dissipating at 6:41 pm near Wausaukee.  Total path length is estimated to be 6.601 miles long, with a width of 200 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 93 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed north of a warm front and tracked east across much of southern WI. A swath of destructive hail and sporadic wind damage occurred. More specifically, vehicle, shingle, siding, gutter, and window screen damage. Short segments of tree damage also occurred. Three people drowned in Milwaukee when two men attempted to help a 10 year old boy who had fallen into the rapidly rising waters of a drainage ditch.

Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the late morning and afternoon of June 15th. The morning storms produced some hail and wind damage. Quarter sized hail fell in Stoddard (Vernon County) and trees were blown down southeast of La Crosse (La Crosse County). The afternoon storms were stronger and produced three tornadoes along with wind damage. An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped in Sextonville.

A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon across western Wisconsin and tracked through northeast Wisconsin through the evening hours on Wednesday (6/15).  The storms formed into a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) with multiple reports of damaging winds of 70 to 80 mph as well as 10 tornadoes embedded within the main line of thunderstorms.  The 10 tornadoes ties a record for the most tornadoes in an event for northeast Wisconsin.  Additional storms late in the evening produced a few reports of large hail near the lakeshore as they moved offshore.  ||The swath of damaging winds caused multiple power outages, with over 125,000 customers without power across northeast Wisconsin.  Power was not restored for several days across many areas as the cleanup from the storms took an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a wind gust to 66 mph at the Wautoma airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed north of a warm front and tracked east across much of southern WI. A swath of destructive hail and sporadic wind damage occurred. More specifically, vehicle, shingle, siding, gutter, and window screen damage. Short segments of tree damage also occurred. Three people drowned in Milwaukee when two men attempted to help a 10 year old boy who had fallen into the rapidly rising waters of a drainage ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the late morning and afternoon of June 15th. The morning storms produced some hail and wind damage. Quarter sized hail fell in Stoddard (Vernon County) and trees were blown down southeast of La Crosse (La Crosse County). The afternoon storms were stronger and produced three tornadoes along with wind damage. An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Stoddard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southwest winds brought hot and humid conditions to southern WI. Heat index values well over 100 degrees occurred. Cooling centers were opened in some communities. Two heat related fatalities occurred in Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat Index values ranging from 105 to 113 degrees on June 14th and rising into the lower 100's once again on June 15th. Milwaukee County Medical Examiner determined that a 39 year old female and a 89 year old male died from heat related causes. The female was found in her house and the gentleman was found collapsed in his backyard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed north of a warm front and tracked east across much of southern WI. A swath of destructive hail and sporadic wind damage occurred. More specifically, vehicle, shingle, siding, gutter, and window screen damage. Short segments of tree damage also occurred. Three people drowned in Milwaukee when two men attempted to help a 10 year old boy who had fallen into the rapidly rising waters of a drainage ditch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and tree limbs down in Aztalan State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed north of a warm front and tracked east across much of southern WI. A swath of destructive hail and sporadic wind damage occurred. More specifically, vehicle, shingle, siding, gutter, and window screen damage. Short segments of tree damage also occurred. Three people drowned in Milwaukee when two men attempted to help a 10 year old boy who had fallen into the rapidly rising waters of a drainage ditch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 year old boy fell into rapidly rising waters of a concrete drainage ditch after a thunderstorm with heavy rainfall. His father and neighbor jumped in to help, but all three were swept away and drowned. The boy was found downstream the next day in the Kinnickinnic River. The other two men were found June 16th in the same river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon across western Wisconsin and tracked through northeast Wisconsin through the evening hours on Wednesday (6/15).  The storms formed into a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) with multiple reports of damaging winds of 70 to 80 mph as well as 10 tornadoes embedded within the main line of thunderstorms.  The 10 tornadoes ties a record for the most tornadoes in an event for northeast Wisconsin.  Additional storms late in the evening produced a few reports of large hail near the lakeshore as they moved offshore.  ||The swath of damaging winds caused multiple power outages, with over 125,000 customers without power across northeast Wisconsin.  Power was not restored for several days across many areas as the cleanup from the storms took an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and powerlines near Coloma. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the late morning and afternoon of June 15th. The morning storms produced some hail and wind damage. Quarter sized hail fell in Stoddard (Vernon County) and trees were blown down southeast of La Crosse (La Crosse County). The afternoon storms were stronger and produced three tornadoes along with wind damage. An EF2 tornado developed south of Tomah (Monroe County) and tracked northeast into the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) before dissipating. This tornado produced extensive tree damage, some structural damage to buildings south of Wyeville (Monroe County) and blew over several semis along Interstate 90/94 resulting in two injuries. Monroe County declared a state of emergency. An EF1 tornado moved across rural portions of Vernon County east of La Farge (Vernon County) causing mainly tree damage. A second EF1 tornado developed very near Mauston (Juneau County) and then tracked through the city before moving northeast and dissipating just west of the Wisconsin River south of Castle Rock Dam. The hospital and several other buildings in Mauston sustained some structural damage, mainly to roofs. Several mobile homes on the east side Mauston were damaged by downed trees from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF2 tornado developed in Monroe County and moved into Juneau County northwest of Cutler. The tornado moved northeast and dissipated in the Necedah Wildlife Refuge. Extensive tree damage occurred along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a weak cold front across southern WI. A couple powerful and slow moving storms affected the Oconomowoc area, producing widespread golf ball sized hail. The hail damage was generally between Concord Road on the west, Highway 67 on the east, just north of Lac La Belle for the northern edge, and I-94 for the southern boundary. The hail lasted for 20-30 minutes and accumulated. Widespread damage to residential homes, businesses, vehicles, and agriculture occurred. More specifically, shingle, siding, downspout, and window screen damage. Some broken windows occurred. Gardens were shredded and trees were stripped of much of their leaves. The hail accumulated and blocked storm sewer runoff, resulting in street flooding. Some basement and business flooding occurred.

A large area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest in the days leading up to the Christmas holiday, bringing a storm with two distinct phases. The first phase occurred on the 21-22nd with widespread snowfall across the region of 3 to 7 inches. Higher amounts to 8.5 inches were received near Webb Lake in Burnett County. Tater phase of the storm featured blizzard conditions in Iron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches was recorded across the county with a high report of 8.5 inches near Webb Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for Western Wisconsin. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system with strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 30-40 mph ended up being about ten miles per hour less than forecast, but these winds together with the arctic air led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F.||The highest reported snowfall amounts were 8 inches in Moesville (Pierce), 7.3 inches near Menomonie (Dunn), and 6.5 inches SE of Hudson (St. Croix).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 4 to 6 inches across the county within a 12-hour period. The highest snowfall amounts were observed near the southern border of the county or about 10 mi NE of New Richmond, WI. This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across parts of southwest Wisconsin on December 8th and 9th. This was the first heavy snowfall of the season. Snowfall amounts of 2 to 8 inches were reported across Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. The highest reported totals were 8.5 inches near Richland Center (Richland County) and 7.5 inches near Mount Sterling (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 2 to 6 inches of snow fell across Grant County. The highest reported total was 6.8 inches in Blue River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the region late December 14th into the morning of December 15th. Snowfall amounts of 2 to 9 inches were reported from western Wisconsin to central Wisconsin, respectively. The highest reported totals were 9.3 inches in Mather (Jackson County), 9.0 inches in Greenwood (Clark County), and 8.5 inches in Tomah (Monroe County). This storm caused several power outages to occur with interruptions  in La Crosse, Juneau, Monroe, Taylor, Trempealeau and Vernon Counties. The largest outage was in La Crosse County where more than 6,700 customers were without power. U.S. Highway 53 was closed near Pidgeon Falls (Trempealeau County) because of downed lines across the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across Clark County. The highest reported total was 9 inches in Greenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final phase of the storm during the days ahead of the Christmas holiday featured blizzard conditions along parts of the South Shore. A second low developed over the Ohio Valley and moved into the region as it deepened. This led to strong northwesterly winds gusting to around 40 mph in the Hurley/Ironwood area. These winds lofted the snow from earlier in this system, along with some light new snow, and created blizzard conditions in the Hurley area for a period during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three hour period of blizzard conditions was recorded at the Hurley-Ironwood Airport during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 23rd. A peak wind gust of 40 mph was recorded during this period. Additionally, a spotter near Gile received 7 inches of new snow accumulation through the day as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to western Wisconsin ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range with the highest totals of 6 inches near Blue River (Grant County) and 5.7 inches in Mondovi (Buffalo County). Strong winds of 25 to 40 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow with some whiteout conditions occurring at times. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -41 at Volk Field (Juneau County). A state patrol officer was injured when another vehicle crashed into the back of a patrol car that was parked along the shoulder of Interstates 90/94 in southern Juneau County. The Governor of Wisconsin declared an energy emergency that allowed suppliers to catch up from weather related delays and for the faster arrival of out of state utility restoration workers. A national food delivery service suspended operations across western Wisconsin during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across Adams  County. The highest reported total was 5.5 inches near Friendship. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to western Wisconsin ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range with the highest totals of 6 inches near Blue River (Grant County) and 5.7 inches in Mondovi (Buffalo County). Strong winds of 25 to 40 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow with some whiteout conditions occurring at times. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -41 at Volk Field (Juneau County). A state patrol officer was injured when another vehicle crashed into the back of a patrol car that was parked along the shoulder of Interstates 90/94 in southern Juneau County. The Governor of Wisconsin declared an energy emergency that allowed suppliers to catch up from weather related delays and for the faster arrival of out of state utility restoration workers. A national food delivery service suspended operations across western Wisconsin during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 4 inches of snow fell across Clark  County. The highest reported total was 5 inches near Owen. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful, long duration winter storm brought widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 24 inches across parts of northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin, with areas around Lake Superior receiving up to 30 inches of snow. A wintry mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow began Tuesday (December 13th) afternoon and evening, spreading northeast overnight Tuesday night and into Wednesday (December 14th) morning. This first wave of precipitation brought a widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow.  We had a break in the snowfall during the day on Wednesday, but it started up again Wednesday night and continued into Thursday (December 15th), bringing another 8-20 inches of snow.  This second wave of precipitation was nearly all snow, enhanced by the main upper level low pressure system as it moved close to the area.  Lighter snow lingered into Friday and Saturday, December 16th and 17th.  A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early Tuesday, moved northeast to eastern Nebraska by Wednesday morning, then to east-central Minnesota by Thursday morning, lingering there through Friday before moving off to the northeast Friday night and Saturday. ||The storm resulted in a long period of poor road conditions, with strong winds causing blowing and drifting snow. There were also widespread power outages, with some people reporting no power for over two days!||Notable with this storm was how wet and heavy the snow was, with 3-day liquid equivalent values of 1 to 4 inches. The wet quality of this snow, unusual for winter storms in this area, heavily weighed down trees and power lines. The density of the snowpack also exacerbated the quality of snow to absorb the red end of the visible light spectrum, transmitting the blue end, which led to many folks observing a glacier blue color in holes and cracks in the snow as they cleared it. Because of this blue color, as well as the very wet quality of the snowfall, NWS Duluth has taken to referring to this snow as the Blue Blizzard of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 10 to 16 inches were reported across Douglas County with a high report of 20 inches in the Hawthorne area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for Western Wisconsin. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios across west central Wisconsin. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system with strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 30-40 mph ended up being about ten miles per hour less than forecast, but these winds together with the arctic air led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F. Roads became ice-covered, but overall the poor conditions did not verify the second half of the winter weather headlines across west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were expected from a deep fresh snow pack and forecast winds of 40 to 50 mph. However, wind gusts ended up being less than forecast, so this event did not verify the Blizzard Warning. Roads were ice-covered, and very cold wind chills of -25 to -35 were observed.

The density of the snowpack also exacerbated the quality of snow to absorb the red end of the visible light spectrum, transmitting the blue end, which led to many folks observing a glacier blue color in holes and cracks in the snow as they cleared it. Because of this blue color, as well as the very wet quality of the snowfall, NWS Duluth has taken to referring to this snow as the Blue Blizzard of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 8 inches were recorded across Iron County with a high report of 9.5 inches near Mercer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for Western Wisconsin. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios across west central Wisconsin. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system with strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 30-40 mph ended up being about ten miles per hour less than forecast, but these winds together with the arctic air led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F. Roads became ice-covered, but overall the poor conditions did not verify the second half of the winter weather headlines across west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were expected from a deep fresh snow pack and forecast winds of 40 to 50 mph. However, wind gusts ended up being less than forecast, so this event did not verify the Blizzard Warning. Roads were ice-covered, and very cold wind chills of -25 to -35 were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to western Wisconsin ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range with the highest totals of 6 inches near Blue River (Grant County) and 5.7 inches in Mondovi (Buffalo County). Strong winds of 25 to 40 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow with some whiteout conditions occurring at times. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -41 at Volk Field (Juneau County). A state patrol officer was injured when another vehicle crashed into the back of a patrol car that was parked along the shoulder of Interstates 90/94 in southern Juneau County. The Governor of Wisconsin declared an energy emergency that allowed suppliers to catch up from weather related delays and for the faster arrival of out of state utility restoration workers. A national food delivery service suspended operations across western Wisconsin during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across La Crosse County. The highest reported total was 3.7 inches near La Crosse. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts. The highest record gust was 40 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin woman passed away from hypothermia. The death likely occurred in late December after the woman was last seen leaving a business in Tomah (Monroe County) during the evening of the 29th. Both air temperatures and wind chill values ranged from the single digits above zero to the 20s over the last few days of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin woman died from hypothermia, likely during the last part of December. The woman was last seen leaving a business in Tomah on the evening of the 29th. Both air temperatures and wind chill values ranged from the single digits above zero to the 20s during the last part of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest in the days leading up to the Christmas holiday, bringing a storm with two distinct phases. The first phase occurred on the 21-22nd with widespread snowfall across the region of 3 to 7 inches. Higher amounts to 8.5 inches were received near Webb Lake in Burnett County. Tater phase of the storm featured blizzard conditions in Iron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches was observed across the county with a high report of 6.1 inches near Mercer.
